I.V. (intravenous) anesthetics typically refer to medications that can be administered directly into the bloodstream, producing rapid onset of action for sedation or general anesthesia. Among the given options, only propofol fits this description as it is commonly used for induction and maintenance of anesthesia through intravenous administration. I.V. (intravenous) anesthetics typically refer to medications that can be administered directly into the bloodstream, producing rapid onset of action for sedation or general anesthesia. Among the given options, only propofol fits this description as it is commonly used for induction and maintenance of anesthesia through intravenous administration. The other options listed—sevoflurane, flumazanil, and naloxone—are not generally delivered via IV route but rather by inhalation or subcutaneous/oral routes respectively. Therefore,

My Answer Choice: A My clinical reasoning indicates that among the provided options, only propofol qualifies as an i.v. anesthetic due to its common use in direct injection for inducing and maintaining general anesthesia. Thus, option A: Propofol is correct.
